claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children would you like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'd like to claim for my two children. I have Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 18.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Since both Alex (17) and Blake (18) are over 16, I'll need to ask a few more questions about their current situations.

**Are Alex and Blake currently in approved education or training?** (This would include things like A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications – but not university or apprenticeships.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in sixth form doing A-levels, but Blake finished school and isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Since Blake is 18 and not in approved education or training, I have a follow-up question:

**Has Blake recently left education or training (within the last 20 weeks), and if so, have they registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake left school a while back and hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a couple more questions to confirm your eligibility.

**Do you live in the UK, and do both Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester actually. And yes, both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

I have one more question to check:

**Does Alex or Blake work 24 hours or more a week, or do either of them receive any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work that much - Alex just focuses on studies and Blake does odd jobs here and there but nothing like 24 hours a week. And no, they don't receive any benefits in their own names.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here is my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

### **Alex (age 17) — ✅ Eligible**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. They are under 20, living with you in the UK, currently in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), and do not work 24+ hours a week or receive benefits in their own right. All eligibility criteria are met.

---

### **Blake (age 18) — ❌ Not Eligible**
Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Since Blake is over 16 and is no longer in approved education or training, they no longer qualify. The option to register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks) typically needs to be done shortly after leaving education, and Blake left school some time ago without registering.

---

### **Summary**
You can claim Child Benefit for **Alex** while they remain in approved education (up until age 20). You are not eligible to claim for **Blake** under the current circumstances.

If Blake were to return to approved education or training in the future, you may be able to make a new claim at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is currently in approved education (A-levels at sixth form). You live in the UK and Alex lives with you, meaning you are responsible for them. Alex does not work 24 hours or more per week and does not receive benefits in their own right. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and is not in approved education or training. Blake left school some time ago and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would have allowed a 20-week extension, but this must be done shortly after leaving education). Since Blake is over 16 and not in approved education or training, and does not qualify for any of the exceptions that allow Child Benefit to continue, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are a UK resident living with both of your children, which establishes your responsibility for them. However, Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16 or over depends on whether they remain in approved education or training. Alex qualifies as they are in sixth form studying A-levels, but Blake does not qualify as they are 18, no longer in education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services within the eligible timeframe.

Blake is 18 years old and is not in approved education or training. Blake left school some time ago and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would have allowed a 20-week extension, but this must be done shortly after leaving education). Since Blake is over 16 and not in approved education or training, and does not qualify for any of the exceptions that allow Child Benefit to continue, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

However, Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16 or over depends on whether they remain in approved education or training. Alex qualifies as they are in sixth form studying A-levels, but Blake does not qualify as they are 18, no longer in education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services within the eligible timeframe.
